topics.title: Integration Testing with Testcontainers
topics.description: Testcontainers is a Java library that spins up real Docker containers during tests — a real MySQL, Redis, Kafka, or any service — and tears them down after. No more mocking the database layer or maintaining shared test servers. Tests run against real infrastructure, catching integration bugs that H2/mocks miss: SQL dialect differences, index behaviour, transaction isolation, schema migration issues.
topics.story: A team mocked their MySQL repository layer in tests. Every test passed. On deployment, the migration failed: they had used H2's AUTO_INCREMENT syntax but MySQL requires AUTO_INCREMENT with a specific engine setting. Three hours of debugging later, they added Testcontainers. Now every test in CI spins up an actual MySQL 8.0 container, runs Flyway migrations, and tests against the real engine. The same migration failure shows up in 30 seconds during `mvn test` — not after a production deployment.
topics.analogy: Unit test = testing in a simulator (fast, isolated, fake controls). Integration test with mocks = testing with fake parts (realistic logic, fake I/O). Integration test with Testcontainers = testing with real parts in a controlled sandbox (real MySQL, real Redis, no shared state). @Testcontainers annotation = start containers before tests. @Container = declare the container. withReuse(true) = reuse container across test classes (faster CI). GenericContainer = any Docker image. MySQLContainer, PostgreSQLContainer, RedisContainer = convenience wrappers.
topics.memoryAnchor: @Testcontainers — JUnit 5 extension. @Container static MySQLContainer<?> mysql = new MySQLContainer<>("mysql:8.0"). @DynamicPropertySource — inject container ports into Spring context. mysql.getJdbcUrl() / getUsername() / getPassword(). @SpringBootTest + @Testcontainers = full integration test. withInitScript("schema.sql") — run SQL on container start. withReuse(true) + testcontainers.reuse.enable=true in ~/.testcontainers.properties — reuse across test runs. KafkaContainer, RedisContainer, LocalStackContainer (AWS).
topics.firstPrinciples: Integration bugs exist in the gap between your code and real infrastructure. H2 is not MySQL — different SQL dialects, different behaviour for edge cases, missing features. Mocking the repository means you never test the actual SQL. Testcontainers solves this by: 1) pulling a Docker image (or using a cached one), 2) starting the container with a random port (avoiding conflicts), 3) exposing the port for your application, 4) running the test, 5) stopping/removing the container. @DynamicPropertySource overrides Spring Boot's `spring.datasource.url` with the container's actual port at runtime — before the ApplicationContext starts.
topics.bruteForce: Shared test database: all developers use a shared staging MySQL. Tests fail randomly due to state left by other developers. Order-dependent tests. CI breaks because the shared DB is down for maintenance. Alternative: H2 in-memory — fast but hides dialect bugs and missing features.
topics.optimizedApproach: Each test class gets its own container (or reuses a running one with withReuse). Tests are isolated: each test method runs in a transaction that rolls back (or the container is reset). No shared state, no MySQL vs H2 dialect differences, catches schema migration issues in CI.
topics.whenToUse: Repository/DAO layer tests with real database queries. Testing Flyway/Liquibase migrations. Kafka consumer/producer integration tests. Redis caching integration tests. Any test where the real infrastructure behaviour matters. NOT for: pure unit tests (overkill), tests where a mock is genuinely sufficient, environments without Docker (though Testcontainers Cloud solves this).

examples.title: Spring Boot + MySQL Testcontainers Integration Test
examples.explanation: @Testcontainers + @Container starts a real MySQL 8.0 Docker container. @DynamicPropertySource injects the container's JDBC URL (with random port) into the Spring context before the ApplicationContext starts. @Transactional rolls back after each test keeping state clean. The unique constraint test catches MySQL-specific behavior that H2 might handle differently. KafkaContainer tests the real Kafka publish/consume cycle.
examples.realWorldUse: A real Spring Boot project has: UserRepositoryTest, OrderRepositoryTest, ProductRepositoryTest — all using the same static MySQLContainer with withReuse(true). The container starts once per JVM run. Tests share it — each @Transactional test rolls back. CI time: 15 seconds to start the container, then all tests run against it. Total test suite: 45 seconds vs 2 minutes for a separate test database.

problems.title: Identify Integration Test Scenarios
problems.description:
Given N test descriptions, classify each as: `UNIT` (no real infrastructure needed), `INTEGRATION_MOCK` (mocks the infrastructure), or `INTEGRATION_REAL` (needs real infrastructure — Testcontainers).

Rules:
- Tests involving `real SQL`, `migration`, `constraint`, `transaction isolation`, `real Kafka`, `real Redis` → INTEGRATION_REAL
- Tests involving `mock`, `stub`, `verify interaction`, `in-memory` → INTEGRATION_MOCK
- Tests involving pure logic, algorithms, transformations with no I/O → UNIT

Sample input:
```
3
test unique email constraint with MySQL
test service logic with mocked repository
test fibonacci calculation
```
Expected:
```
INTEGRATION_REAL
INTEGRATION_MOCK
UNIT
```
problems.hint1: Check for keywords: constraint/migration/real SQL/real Kafka/real Redis → INTEGRATION_REAL.
problems.testCases:
[{"input":"3
test unique email constraint with MySQL
test service logic with mocked repository
test fibonacci calculation","expectedOutput":"INTEGRATION_REAL
INTEGRATION_MOCK
UNIT"},{"input":"2
run database migration
verify stub was called","expectedOutput":"INTEGRATION_REAL
INTEGRATION_MOCK"},{"input":"1
test sorting algorithm","expectedOutput":"UNIT"}]
problems.sampleInput:
3
test unique email constraint with MySQL
test service logic with mocked repository
test fibonacci calculation
problems.editorial: The testing pyramid: Unit tests are fast and isolated. Integration tests with mocks are faster but can miss real infrastructure bugs. Integration tests with Testcontainers are slower but catch production-like issues. The key insight: only use Testcontainers when real infrastructure behavior matters (SQL dialects, constraints, Kafka ordering, Redis TTL). For pure business logic, unit tests are faster and easier to maintain.

problems.title: Container Startup Order Dependency
problems.description:
Given N container start events with dependencies (container B depends on container A = B must start after A), determine a valid startup order using topological sort.

Input:
- First line: N (containers), M (dependencies)
- Next N lines: container name
- Next M lines: `A B` meaning B depends on A (A starts first)

Output: valid startup order (any valid topological sort).

Sample input:
```
4 3
mysql
redis
app
nginx
mysql app
redis app
app nginx
```
Expected (one valid order): `mysql redis app nginx`
problems.hint1: Build a directed graph: edge A→B means A must come before B.
problems.hint2: Use Kahn's algorithm: start with all nodes with in-degree 0.
problems.hint3: Process queue: add node to result, decrement neighbors' in-degree, add newly zero-degree nodes to queue.
problems.testCases:
[{"input":"4 3
mysql
redis
app
nginx
mysql app
redis app
app nginx","expectedOutput":"mysql redis app nginx"},{"input":"2 1
db
app
db app","expectedOutput":"db app"},{"input":"3 0
a
b
c","expectedOutput":"a b c"}]
problems.sampleInput:
4 3
mysql
redis
app
nginx
mysql app
redis app
app nginx
problems.editorial: Testcontainers with depends_on semantics: your app container needs the DB to be healthy before starting. Kahn's algorithm finds a valid topological order. In Testcontainers, this is handled via container.waitingFor(Wait.forHealthcheck()) and @Container ordering. The topological sort ensures no container starts before its dependencies are ready.

problems.title: Test Isolation: Detect Shared State Bugs
problems.description:
Given test execution logs showing state modifications, determine if any test leaves state that affects subsequent tests (isolation violation).

Each test line: `TEST name: READ key=val WRITE key=val` or `TEST name: READ key=val` or `TEST name: WRITE key=val`.

State starts empty. A test that WRITEs a key and a later test that READs the same key has an isolation violation.

Print `VIOLATION: testB reads key written by testA` for each violation. If none, print `OK`.

Sample:
```
3
TEST t1: WRITE user=alice
TEST t2: READ user=alice
TEST t3: WRITE order=1
```
Expected: `VIOLATION: t2 reads user written by t1`
problems.hint1: Parse each line to extract test name and WRITE/READ pairs.
problems.hint2: Maintain a Map<key, testName> tracking which test last wrote each key.
problems.hint3: When a test READs a key that was WRITten by a different test, it's a violation.
problems.testCases:
[{"input":"3
TEST t1: WRITE user=alice
TEST t2: READ user=alice
TEST t3: WRITE order=1","expectedOutput":"VIOLATION: t2 reads user written by t1"},{"input":"2
TEST t1: WRITE x=1
TEST t1: READ x=1","expectedOutput":"OK"},{"input":"1
TEST t1: READ user=bob","expectedOutput":"OK"}]
problems.sampleInput:
3
TEST t1: WRITE user=alice
TEST t2: READ user=alice
TEST t3: WRITE order=1
problems.sampleOutput: VIOLATION: t2 reads user written by t1
problems.editorial: Test isolation is critical: each test must start with a clean slate. Without @Transactional rollback or container reset, data written by t1 leaks into t2. Testcontainers solves this at the container level. @Transactional in Spring Boot test rolls back after each @Test method. This problem models the bug that happens when isolation is broken — t2's result depends on t1's side effects, making tests order-dependent and flaky.
